Name: informationtheoreticpruningviaadaptivecoding
Title: Adaptive Information-Theoretic Pruning via Rate-Distortion Theory
Short Hypothesis: Can we design an adaptive pruning algorithm that dynamically allocates bits to different layers of a neural network based on their task-relevant information content, using rate-distortion theory?
Related Work: Existing work in model pruning and compression (e.g., Isik et al., 2021; Bu et al., 2019) has focused on static pruning strategies and information-theoretic bounds. However, these methods do not adaptively allocate bits to different layers based on their current information content. This proposal aims to bridge this gap by developing an adaptive pruning algorithm that uses rate-distortion theory to dynamically allocate bits.
Abstract: We propose an adaptive pruning algorithm for neural network compression that dynamically allocates bits to different layers based on their task-relevant information content. Unlike existing methods that use static pruning strategies, our approach leverages rate-distortion theory to adaptively allocate bits, ensuring that the most informative layers are preserved while less important layers are pruned. We derive a theoretical framework for this adaptive allocation, using mutual information and entropy estimators to quantify the information content of each layer. We validate our method on standard benchmarks, demonstrating superior compression rates while maintaining or even improving model performance.
Experiments: Derive a theoretical framework for adaptive bit allocation using rate-distortion theory, including the derivation of achievable rates and distortion measures.
Implement the adaptive pruning algorithm, using mutual information estimators (e.g., InfoNCE) to measure the information content of each layer.
Conduct experiments on standard datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet) to compare the compression rates and model performance of our method against static pruning strategies.
Analyze the bit allocation patterns across different layers to understand how the algorithm adapts to the information content of the network.
Evaluate the robustness of the algorithm to different hyperparameters and initial conditions.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The mutual information estimators used in the adaptive pruning algorithm may introduce biases or inaccuracies, affecting the bit allocation.
The computational cost of the adaptive pruning algorithm may be higher than static pruning methods, particularly for large-scale models.
The theoretical framework may not generalize to all types of neural networks or tasks, and may require specific adjustments for different architectures.
The adaptive bit allocation may lead to suboptimal solutions if the initial conditions or hyperparameters are not carefully chosen.
